item.content:encoded: Thanks everyone for making this happen holy cow this hit +100!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this, but a few things caught my attention,

+ Open
- Close

First in Exploration Log 1 I feel that it needs to be made clear early that the hangar and surrounding areas that the team is exploring is not underwater, because I initially assumed that they are submerged given that they are deep underwater and entered through a hangar for underwater vehicles. If they are submerged in water than it is difficult to tell.
Another thought too is in the preface for Exploration Log 1 could mention that the recon team included a __ man tactical team, because they were part of the crew.

At this time, it was noted that tactical teams were not equipped with thaumotologic weaponry.

This statement seems odd as it seems out of the blue, are the tactical team supposed to have thaumotologic weaponry?

Also, some of the descriptive text that are inside the brackets appear to be a bit informal and somewhat lose the clinical tone, examples:

The sub is visible as R-6 rotates to capture the interior, the back of the vehicle is breached, and the remainder crushed together as if it were a tin can.
Numerous ominous lights are visibly approaching.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been thinking about this critcism a lot recently and you make a fair number of valid points… I think I'm going to return to this in the coming months and touch things up. I'll probably contact you via pm when I do Psul.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I can forgive a certain amount of action-movie cliche when aiming for something epic, this had a constant stream of immersion-breaking details that led me to downvote. It could really do with more attention to detail and logic.

Just a couple of examples:

  • the Finnfolk speak in English, with Norwegian accents. Not Norwegian, but modern English with a recognisably Norwegian accent?
  • the recon team is instructed to fortify their position and await specialist reinforcements. They do so. Then they are instructed to leave their fortified position before the reinforcements arrive.
  • Queen Hege says “I’m not going to sit on the sidelines” - why this modern English idiom? Do the Finnfolk understand sports played on fields?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This started off a lot slower than I normally am comfortable with, but the story is so expertly crafted that my attention was held from start to finish. While I understand that some readers may take issue with the lack of clinical tone in the logs, the length, or the characters themselves, I personally have no issue with any of that. These events happened, and they need to be adequately described in a manner that anybody reading the article for the first time with no debrief would be able to picture what went down.

I do have one minor complaint though. I didn't like the way that the newspaper articles were included, breaking up the logs like they do. I suggest instead having the logs as collapsibles with increasing clearance requirements, as well as putting the recovered documents in their own collapsible together.

Well earned +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1777 AD

Magisters investigating claim the source of the fire was a match which accidentally ignited imbibes in the basement. The child, one Skreyja Holgata, has been moved to a proper home.

The self-igniting match was invented in 1805.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So a couple of things, since I can't really grok with your feedback.

Firstly, the exploration logs read like episodes of Scooby Doo. The scene where they accidentally activate a surround sound system playing tribal music thus alerting the guards is the worst offender

I'm wondering why you picked the phrase "tribal music" here (it's explicitly described as electronic), but contrived coincidences is kinda what fiction runs on.

I've mentioned before that really long articles with lots of logs are hard to pull off, since you need to hold the reader's attention for a long time, and this article fails miserably in that regard.

A significant number of veteran authors and readers disagrees with this assessment. This was a very long article, that held our attention in spite of its length.

I had already lost interest by the first exploration log, and by the hour mark I just wanted it to end.

This…may indicate more of the issue. It took you over an hour to read ~10-15 pages of writing? This is 14k words, yes, but a lot of that is formatting.

I'm not sure why your personal consumption of this took so long, but I can imagine spending that long on any article feels tiring. I don't think your criticisms land particularly well, though, because you didn't actually explain your issues beyond "I don't like coincidences" and "It's long". That's enough for a downvote, but the phrasing of your feedback is …frustrating as a long-time reader and author, because it comes off as dismissive for a piece that definitely can't be summed up so easily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was painful to read in multiple ways.

I didn't comment on this originally because I was having trouble putting my thoughts into words, but now that I know what I want to say, I can leave my critique. Firstly, the exploration logs read like episodes of Scooby Doo. The scene where they accidentally activate a surround sound system playing tribal music thus alerting the guards is the worst offender, but there are several others, such as all of them being caught in a single net placed on a forest path. This is also so fucking long. I've mentioned before that really long articles with lots of logs are hard to pull off, since you need to hold the reader's attention for a long time, and this article fails miserably in that regard. I had already lost interest by the first exploration log, and by the hour mark I just wanted it to end.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this and all of your Finnfolk related stuff, which I ended up bingereading.
Despite being a very long article, I never once got bored reading this due to the depth of the story. That, and the fact that I love underwater scifi and good worldbuilding.
Bravo, I wish I could upvote this more than once, but since I can't, I'll just have to settle giving you a well deserved +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This criticism is fair and appreciated. I'm going to do my best to adjust and fix the pieces that you've pointed out made this less enjoyable. I've already made a massive change to the ending to remove the perceived Deus Ex Machina. As is, the recovered materials are not something I can fix as immediately, as I will have to carefully consider how to diversify and improve the individual pieces of recovered material.

Show Response to Criticism
Hide Response

In order to shed light on portions of this, I feel it necessary to explain some of the motivation behind pieces that were included. The recovered materials were constructed in order to give the reader space to breath between the logs. You are the first, though I'm sure not the only, person to leave feedback in a way that indicates this was problematic, and/or you didn't enjoy the story being woven by the logs, which is fair.

They are, somewhat heavily, leaning towards the side of exposition, which is a failure on my part. At the time that they were written, they were meant to serve the breathing room purpose and the primary vector for the history. I could perhaps have improved upon that structure, with more personalized documents and recovered materials, personal items, etc. Collected by the Foundation as they are exploring, to more indirectly/less expositionally demonstrate the events leading to the state the Finnfolk are currently in. This comes down to a lack of time, and a lack of necessary direction to see the exposition-heavy and lukewarm nature of the materials.

I understand the dialogue complaint, cause its a fairly common one on the site. I don't entirely agree with the criticism, especially in the face of a team of experienced agents who've, as to put it, "been through the shitter." The characters are written the way they are because they're human. Because they've been through hell and how they cope with it, as a group that has chemistry, is by bantering. I went so far as to directly address this, in a not so subtle manner.

R-2: "Of course we do, just look out at the city. It's all a big angry problem."

R-Cap: "Roger command, we're reading." [To R-2] "Jane, cut the sarcasm to command. Save the banter for the field."

The first example you provided was a suggestion made to inject more humor into the article, the second example has been praised by a number of different authors and users, and the last leads into a vicious response to a lack of respect on the part of R-5. I will probably do more editing to the most offending pieces of dialogue, but I'm relatively unwilling to budge on the bantering/somewhat loose and unprofessional nature of the Recon team members, or the eccentric behavior of Ragnhild, as those elements have received very positive responses from a spread of different users, and I'm afraid if I remove them, the logs will fall victim to the same issue the incident log from 3456 fell into.

With that said,

On top of that, each of these logs reads like a tale. Had you made them their own tale series I think it might have worked out a little more in your favor, as then you could really get into imagery to describe your world and bring it to life.

This is the shortcoming of the medium, and obviously a pitfall of logs in general, but also a result of attempting to restrain my desire to describe every succinct detail (Knowing what is and isn't important, and being able to discern and prioritize elements over others is something I struggle with not just in writing, but everywhere. I've gotten into the habit of describing less rather than more). Several times, while I was giving feedback, Roget suggested small tweaks to give the reader a better visualization of what was happening/improve upon the imagery. I would love to know if there are specific spots or locations in the logs where I could do that in order to provide the reader with a better image of what is happening, and thus, better engage them. Clinical tone isn't the best medium for conveying imagery, but setting the stage in the right manner, its possible to paint a beautiful mental image, and any guidance therein where those images can be improved is appreciated.

Finally, in regards to the image, describing the nature of the nested cylinders proved extremely difficult to do without visual aides. I hate that it broke immersion for you, I was unsure how else I could help the reader visualize the space, and chose to include an image of what it would, theoretically, most closely resemble. If enough people found it immersion breaking I can certainly remove it.

I greatly appreciate the in-depth feedback, there are changes here that I can make and things I can improve on. I hate that you didn't enjoy it, and feel as if a number of these things I can fix and improve upon following the end of the 4000 contest. I've made significant efforts to fix the article's ending, and remove the Deus Ex-Machina, and replace it with a climactic battle, calling back to 3700, while demonstrating the capabilities of the machine that the entire article has built towards. It fixes one thing… and hopefully, I can fix the rest.

This response got long, man, I just write too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Effective worldbuilding in an article can be a beautiful thing. It can make the world of the SCP Foundation come to life and seem like a real place, with rules, history, and its own clashes and ideals. This is what makes pieces like WrongJohnSilver's 001 or the TwistedGears/djkaktus 001 so effective. Poor worldbuilding, on the other hand, typically ends up being exposition dumps. While they often do establish their own world, they bog themselves down and lose the reader in the resulting quagmire. I'm afraid this article was the latter for me.

+ show block
– hide block

The object itself is fairly interesting. A massive combat sub built into a giant hermit crab is quite a spectacle to behold, and I do like the first diagram explaining the various layers of the interior. This gives the article some depth and makes the object seem like an explored place. The second picture breaks my immersion a little, since I've seen that illustration around the net before, and I hesitate to believe it would be included in an SCP document.

Then we get down to the supporting documents. The News Articles after a while just blend into a long exposition dump. You have an entire civilization discovered and the only interesting/noteworthy documents the SCP Foundation can find are newspapers and maybe one or two other document styles? On top of that, the history you tell here is a little lukewarm to me as a reader. Perhaps I would find more fondness here if I was a little more invested in your Finnfolk canon, but as is going through those is a bit like watching a Star Wars movie and pausing every now and then to read an ongoing history about the Hutts. While I am sure that will draw some fans, it's not the reason I am here to see the film.

The exploration logs prove problematic in their own way. With dialogue gems like:

R-5: "I got a bad feeling about all of this."

R-3: "You get bad feelings about everything."

R-5: "They usually aren't wrong!"

R-3: "That's because you're a walking cliche 90% of the time."

R-2: "At the end of pointy sticks again."

R-Cap: "Reminds me of that mission in Micronesia."

R-5: "Aren't we not supposed to talk about that?"

R-Cap: "You aren't."

R-6: "Who are you?"

Matriarch: "I'm Ragnhild the Mad." [Five seconds of cackling.]

R-5: "I'll say."

These read more like the kind of things people might say to their friends while gathered around to play Dungeons and Dragons than believable dialogue from hardened MTF agents, or the elders of ancient and powerful civilizations. It just kills the immersion. On top of that, each of these logs reads like a tale. Had you made them their own tale series I think it might have worked out a little more in your favor, as then you could really get into imagery to describe your world and bring it to life. As is, however, the clinical tone and format of a log suck out that opportunity and we are just left with the dialogue and some dry description of actions.

Finally, you fall prey to Deus Ex Machina at the end. You build up the monsters of SCP-3700 quite a bit in that article and really do a good job justifying the Hiemal object class. Having one of them attack at the end here is a bold move, but instead of making use of the massive battle sub you have spent nearly 12K words building up, you have an outside force swoop in to save the day.

I really want to like this. I can tell a lot of blood, sweat, and tears was poured into it to bring it to life. Unfortunatley, the problems listed above just cause the whole thing to fail to solidfy, and the length does the rest to kill the experience. At +44 at the time of writing, its clear you have an audience here on the site for the article, but I don't think I'm part of it.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad I was able to hit a chord with both you and other readers. It rejuvenates my creative drive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely more streamlined and overall satisfying than the original version, which I had enjoyed to begin with. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Keep em coming
I didn't even know the orcadian Canon and this got me very very interested

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The reception to this article has made me ecstatic, and comments like this breathe life into my creative motivation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Comments like this make me really happy because it means I captured the characters in their element.

These folks have been through so much, they're rarely surprised anymore. And after spending a solid week inside this giant vehicle and fighting Nuckelavees and Trows, its like "Oh there's a dragon now? Of course there is, cause that's the totally logical conclusion to any fantasy epic."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This counts as "lore"
It's long, it's far reaching, it's connected and it's complex
I'm happy to have read it
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the escalation of the fantastic, with the squad never breaking character, so to speak.

Oh, there's a dragon? Of course there is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed it all! And I'm really glad you enjoyed that bit, every piece of dialogue here was fun to write, and took lots of thought and effort to hammer out (which is why it took me 3 weeks to write this monster.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple things that I noticed:

"Skreyja Sends us not to Glory, but to Die. We all go to [Coordinates Redacted] until the beacons are lit.]

No closing quotes.

from which a plasma like substance flows into the air.

Should be "plasma-like" or "plasmic".

R-Cap: "Reminds me of that mission in Micronesia."

R-5: "Aren't we not supposed to talk about that?"

R-Cap: "You aren't."

Not a criticism, I just loved this bit so much.

Overall, very well done. I thoroughly enjoyed it from beginning to end :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just made a final polishing edit pass, which should have cleaned up any remaining grammar/spelling errors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thanks for the catches, I'll get those in the last polishing edits that I'm making today!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this. Noticed a few possible errors here and there:

"toiroid" -> toroid
"humanoids are assaulted varying anomalies" -> assaulted BY varying?
"to take back the homes of our brothers and sisters if what Grindwald is true" -> if what Grindwald SAID is true?
"and another Finnfolk goes down as two clogs up the vehicles engines" -> two CLOG up? two X clog up?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cannot believe you kept my attention so long. Exquisite. Also, it should be notching their bows not knocking their bows. I want to know the rest of the terms of the Finnfolk-Foundation agreement. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really enjoyed this, the dialogue and characters were well-written and engrossing and it's easy to fall immersed into the world you've crafted here. Very hearty +1 for good shell times.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This was a massive endeavor which started all the way back last June, and has culminated in what's now here on the page.

For crit of the Original version, and completely rebuilt version:

  • Roget
  • Modern_Erasmus
  • The Great Hippo For his log formatting.
  • Tufto Huge help on vetting the early drafts and portions.
  • Anborough
  • Pedantique
  • Weryllium Huge help on vetting the early drafts and individual sections
  • SpookyPizza
  • BananaRepublic for reading the entire draft.
  • Sirens
  • Jacob Conwell
  • MaliceAforethought For reading through bits and pieces of the draft even though he was super busy.
  • Dr0Shadow
  • Mortos
  • taylor_itkin
  • djkaktus For the critt on the original version before I rebuilt it which drove me down the path of focusing more heavily on the characters.
  • Captain Kirby For reading over the entire draft and finding lots of little things to fix.
  • stormfallen For being picky as hell
  • A Random Day For encouragement, and just all around being a great wellspring for bouncing ideas off of, as well as helping me pick out what was and wasn't necessary for this to work.
  • Dog Teeth
  • ProcyonLotor For his critt on the original draft
  • Quikngruvn for the "Algal tea and Candy" idea.

There were a ton of people who critted and looked at this over its development, and I'm sure I've left people out. If I forgot you, just send me a message and I'll fix it.

Image Credits:

The picture of the Crab is released under CC-BY-2.0 and can be found here https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coconut_crab#/media/File:Birgus_latro.jpg

The picture of the O'Neil Cylinder is a public domain image originally released by Nasa. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/O%27Neill_cylinder#/media/File:Spacecolony3edit.jpeg

The cutaway diagram of the cylinders was manually made by me, and is released under CC-BY-3.0. I will provide the name of the program and a link to it once I remember it.

The following collapsible has a bunch of extra stuff.

+ show block
– hide block

Recommended listening for this article:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kZsbtOZWG4o - Breath of the Wild Trailer Orchestral Cover No SFX. Song progression is what I based the feeling of the SCP on. From quiet

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6vCxBQy2SOk - Two Steps from Hell Enchantress
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tGh4FcZKekA - Two Steps from Hell High C's
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aoQApwEk6Uw - Two Steps from Hell Pegasus
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SxThZpslbhE - Thomas Bergerson Into Darkness
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lzU26jUnE7k - Thomas Bergerson Dragonland
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ay3EwNGM5yw - Thomas Bergerson Sun
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iArgqkwlza8 - Thomas Bergerson Threnody for Europe

So there are a bunch of Easter eggs within the SCP I want to play on.

Inference:

  • Big implication that's never actually said in article is that SCP-3700-2 caused the huge hole in the crab.

Names:
Guð-Bani - Old Norse for God Slayer
Skreyja - Old Norse for Incompetent
Aske - Bastardization of Old Norse for Forebear
Hege - Old Norse for Divine Woman
Teran - Orkney for terrible father, or Tyrant. Deity in Orkney folklore
Finnfolk - A race of mer-people in Orkney folklore known for their deviousness and shape shifting capabilities.
Ragnhild - Council of Battle
Brynhild - fighter
Grinwald - Green wood forest
